coredata.dc:title: The carbon dots based on Nile red: The preparation and application to probe α-synuclein fibrils
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qin-Ying
Ma, Li-Yun
Li, Li
Zhang, Jin-Tao
Li, Xi
Zhang, Yu
Jiang, Ming
Luo, Wan-Chun
Yang, Lin-Jiao
Li, Zhi
Huang, Kun
Yu, Xu
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: The aggregation of α-synuclein often links to the occurrence and development of Parkinson’s disease (PD). It is crucial to detect the α-synuclein fibril or monitor the aggregation of monomer for pharmacological and pathological researches, drug development and clinical diagnosis, etc. In this study, the Nile red (NR)-based carbon dots (NR-CDs) were meticulously designed and prepared. The NR-CDs were sensitive to the microenvironment, exhibiting enhanced fluorescence intensity with the elevated viscosity and decreased polarity. Thus, the NR-CDs exhibited high affinity towards α-synuclein fibril with remarkable red fluorescence enhancement, but did not respond to its monomer. Hence, the NR-CDs were used as the turn-on fluorescence probe to detect and image α-synuclein fibrils both in vitro and in vivo, as well as to monitor the aggregation process of the amyloids. Superior to NR dye, NR-CDs were characteristic of lower toxicity, better water dispersibility and anti-photobleaching resistance. In addition, the NR-CDs were demonstrated to inhibit the aggregation of α-synuclein, holding the potential as the therapeutic agent. The present study provided an effective way of designing the CDs using the small molecular dye as the carbon source to improve the biocompatibility and detectability, while reserve the selective turn-on fluorescence towards amyloid aggregates. This strategy may be generalized to construct more fluorescence probes with desired properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: α-synuclein
Nile red
Carbon dots
Parkinson’s disease
Amyloid protein aggregation
Turn-on fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence response of NR-CDs towards amyloid monomers and fibrils
Monitoring amyloid fibrillation process by NR-CDs
Investigation of the inhibition effect of NR-CDs on the aggregation of α-syn
Probing of different amyloid fibrils and monomers by NR-CDs in vitro
Optimization of probing conditions towards α-syn-F by NR-CDs
Mechanism of turn-on fluorescence probing fibrils by NR-CDs
In vitro monitoring aggregation of α-syn using NR-CDs as the probe
Investigation of the inhibition effect of NR-CDs on amyloid aggregation

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82373834 & 22174049). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the material characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The fluorescence response to α-syn and α-syn-F (10 μM) of multiple CDs prepared as listed in Table S1 under (A) different amounts of citric acid (0.02–1.00 g) and (B) different reaction time (4–12 h).

Characterization of NR-CDs. (A) The TEM image of NR-CDs (Insets: the particle size distribution and high-resolution TEM image of NR-CDs). (B) FT-IR spectra of NR-CDs, NR and citric acid. (C-I) Full scan, (C-II) C1s, (C-III) O1s and (C-IV) N1s XPS spectra of NR-CDs.
The fluorescence spectra of (A) NR and (B) NR-CDs (14 μg/mL) towards different amyloid proteins monomers and fibrils (10 μM).

Fluorescence spectra of NR-CDs (A and B) and NR (C and D) in water-glycerol binary solvents system simulating different viscosities and different solvents representing different polarities (NR-CDs: λex=545 nm; NR: λex=530 nm).
Kinetic curves of α-syn aggregation traced by NR-CDs and ThT.
(A) Kinetic curves of α-syn alone and co-incubated with NR-CDs (α-syn + NR-CDs) traced by ThT. TEM images of (B) α-syn alone and (C)α-syn + NR-CDs at the final time point of the incubation.
Fluorescence images of SH-SY5Y cells labeled by NR-CDs (14 μg/mL).
Fluorescence images of NL5901 C. elegans labeled by NR-CDs (scale bar: 100 μm).

head.ce:title: The carbon dots based on Nile red: The preparation and application to probe α-synuclein fibrils

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmacy, Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430022, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These two authors contribute to this work equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aggregation of α-synuclein often links to the occurrence and development of Parkinson’s disease (PD). It is crucial to detect the α-synuclein fibril or monitor the aggregation of monomer for pharmacological and pathological researches, drug development and clinical diagnosis, etc. In this study, the Nile red (NR)-based carbon dots (NR-CDs) were meticulously designed and prepared. The NR-CDs were sensitive to the microenvironment, exhibiting enhanced fluorescence intensity with the elevated viscosity and decreased polarity. Thus, the NR-CDs exhibited high affinity towards α-synuclein fibril with remarkable red fluorescence enhancement, but did not respond to its monomer. Hence, the NR-CDs were used as the turn-on fluorescence probe to detect and image α-synuclein fibrils both in vitro and in vivo, as well as to monitor the aggregation process of the amyloids. Superior to NR dye, NR-CDs were characteristic of lower toxicity, better water dispersibility and anti-photobleaching resistance. In addition, the NR-CDs were demonstrated to inhibit the aggregation of α-synuclein, holding the potential as the therapeutic agent. The present study provided an effective way of designing the CDs using the small molecular dye as the carbon source to improve the biocompatibility and detectability, while reserve the selective turn-on fluorescence towards amyloid aggregates. This strategy may be generalized to construct more fluorescence probes with desired properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The abnormal aggregation of amyloid proteins usually leads to neurodegenerative diseases or metabolic diseases, e.g. Parkinson's disease (PD), Alzheimer's disease (AD) and type II diabetes mellitus (T2DM) [1–4]. The amyloid proteins usually transit from the soluble monomers to oligomers, finally aggregate into the fibrils and deposit in human tissues or organs, causing irreversible damage to human beings [5,6]. The occurrence of the diseases usually accompanies with the appearance of amyloid fibrils, which are now recognized as the pathological markers for clinical diagnosis. Thus, it is of great significance to develop effective detection methods for amyloid aggregates, aiming to get a deep understanding of the mechanism of protein aggregation, early diagnosis and intervention of diseases, as well as drug development.

PD is a neurodegenerative disease, afflicting tens of thousands of people every year with the major symptoms of tremor, bradykinesia, rigor and postural instability [7,8]. It has been claimed that the abnormal aggregation of α-synuclein (α-syn) should be blamed for the PD [1,3,4,9]. The α-syn consists of 140 amino acids and binds to the cell membrane in an α-helical conformation [3,10]. Under the pathological conditions, the monomers aggregate to form insoluble amyloid fibrils. Since the fibrils could propagate through “cell-to-cell” mechanism, one characteristics of the PD is the α-syn fibrils (α-syn-F) deposit in the interconnected brain regions, which is recognized as the hallmark of the disease [10]. However, the mechanism of how this protein causes disease still remains unclear. Thus, it is highly desired of the proper detection method for α-syn-F for pharmacological and pathological researches.

Nowadays, various methods have been developed to detect the amyloid fibrils, i.e. transmission electron microscope (TEM) [11], circular dichroism [12], fluorescence sensors [13–15], et al. Among them, the fluorescence sensors, a method of qualitative or quantitative analysis of the analyte based on the change in the fluorescence wavelength or intensity when the probes interact with the target, are usually praised for their high efficiency, cost-effectiveness and low invasion, holding the potential for fibril detection [16,17]. The two popular classical fluorescence probes to detect the amyloid fibrils in vitro include thioflavin T (ThT) and Congo red [18,19]. ThT is the gold standard probe for amyloid fibrils, but it emits blue-green fluorescence, which is unsuitable for in vivo imaging. For Congo red, the good water solubility facilitates its usage in vitro and the high signal-to-noise ratio is exciting in tissue staining. However, the serious toxicity and poor blood-brain barrier permeability hinder its application in vivo. Hence, it is still desirable to develop an efficient fluorescence probe for amyloid proteins detection, especially for in vivo imaging.

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a new fluorescence nanoprobe to detect the amyloid fibrils with the advantages of low cost, rapid response and adjustable optical properties [20,21]. However, CDs targeting the α-syn-F is still rare. The main possible reasons could be that: (1) there is no reliable and applicable strategy for designing probes targeting the α-syn-F; (2) although there are many methods to regulate the fluorescence properties of CDs, it is still a challenge to obtain red fluorescence or near-infrared emitting CDs for in vivo imaging. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop red fluorescence emitting CDs to effectively probe the α-syn-F.

In our previous study, Congo red was adopted as a carbon source to generate a series of CDs [22]. Compared to Congo red dye, the resulting CDs possessed improved anti-photo bleaching property and biocompatibility, while still retained the high selectivity to the fibrils and succeeded in imaging amyloid fibrils in cells. The study fully demonstrated that it was an effective approach to inherit and improve the properties of the small molecular dye via generating CDs using the dye as the carbon source. As our continuous endeavor to developing fluorescence probes for amyloid fibrils detection, in this study, an uncharged phenoxazone dye, Nile red (NR), was adopted as the co-carbon source with citric acid to generate red fluorescence-emitting CDs (NR-CDs). NR is lipophilic and sensitive to the microenvironment, which was originally used to monitor β-galactosidase aggregation or lysozyme amyloid protein formation in vitro [23–26]. Though NR could bind with the hydrophobic β-sheet of amyloid fibrils with turn-on red fluorescence, its poor water solubility seriously hinders its use both in vitro or in vivo [23]. Therefore, to improve its water solubility and biocompatibility, NR was herein nano-derivatized to be NR-CDs via a one-step preparation, in which NR was supposed to recognize the fibrils and contribute to the fluorescence response. Apart from detection of amyloid fibrils, more applicability of NR-CDs was successfully explored, e.g. monitoring the fibrillation process, inhibiting the protein aggregation and imaging α-syn aggregation. Encouragingly, this study provided a new idea to design multi-functional CDs for detecting and intervening amyloid aggregation.

The NR-CDs were synthesized via a one-pot solvothermal approach. Generally, 0.21 g citric acid was dissolved in 1 mL deionized water and mixed with 4 mL ethanol (EtOH) containing 1 mM NR, which was then transferred into a Teflon-line autoclave of 25 mL and heated at 180℃ for the prescribed time. Afterwards, the obtained solution was cooled down naturally to the room temperature, which was then subjected to dialysis in a 500 Da dialysis bag using water as the dialysate for 12 h to remove the unreacted reagents. Thus-obtained purified solution was kept at 4℃ for further use.

In order to obtain the optimal CDs in terms of turn-on fluorescence response towards α-syn-F, the preparation conditions, e.g. different citric acid amounts (0.02–1.00 g) and reaction time (4–12 h) at a fixed NR concentration (1 mM, in 4 mL EtOH), were investigated as listed in Table S1. The NR-CDs were optimized as the probe for the subsequent use, with the maximum excitation (λmax ex) and emission wavelengths (λmax em) of 545 nm and 630 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of NR-CDs towards amyloid monomers and fibrils
ce:section.ce:para: NR-CDs (140 μg/mL, 30 μL) were dispersed in 240 μL phosphate buffer solution (PB, 10 mM, pH 7.4) with the addition of 30 μL amyloid monomers or fibrils (100 μM), i.e. insulin monomers (ins), insulin fibrils (ins-F), lysozyme monomers (lys), lysozyme fibrils (lys-F), Aβ42 monomers (Aβ), Aβ fibrils (Aβ-F), α-syn and α-syn-F, and the fluorescence spectra were scanned.

To optimize the fluorescence probing performance of NR-CDs towards α-syn-F, the concentration of NR-CDs was investigated. Specifically, 30 μL α-syn-F (100 μM) was dispersed into 240 μL PB (10 mM, pH 7.4), followed by the addition of 30 μL NR-CDs of different concentrations (14, 28, 140, 700, 1400 μg/mL). Then, the samples were subjected to the fluorescence intensity measurement (λmax ex=545 nm, λmax em=630 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring amyloid fibrillation process by NR-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
NR-CDs were employed as the probe to monitor the fibrillation process of α-syn, using ThT as the control. In detail, 100 μM α-syn was incubated at 37℃ with rotary shaking for 7 d. During the incubation, aliquots of the incubation system were sucked out at fixed time intervals and divided into two parts. Each part (30 μL) was added with 240 μL PB (10 mM, pH 7.4) and 30 μL NR-CDs (140 μg/mL) or ThT (100 μM), and then subjected to the fluorescence intensity measurement (ThT: λex = 430 nm, λem = 488 nm; NR-CDs: λmax ex= 545 nm, λmax em= 630 nm). The sigmoid curves of the fibrillation were fitted using the Eq. (1), and the lag time (t lag) was calculated according to the Eq. (2) [20]:
(1) y = y0 + (ymax-y0)/[1+exp-(t-t1/2)k]
(2) tlag = t1/2-2/k

Wherein, y represents the fluorescence intensity recorded at the time of t; y
0 and y
max signify the fluorescence intensity of the probe alone and the maximum fluorescence intensity of the probe while interacting with the fibrils, respectively. t 1/2 denotes the time required for the fluorescence intensity to reach 50% of the maximum, and k represents the rate constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the inhibition effect of NR-CDs on the aggregation of α-syn
ce:section.ce:para: NR-CDs (140 μg/mL) were co-incubated with α-syn (50 μM) at 37℃ with rotary shaking for 7 d. The co-incubation solution was sampled at the prescribed time intervals, and then diluted by 10 mM PB (pH 7.4) of ten-fold volume with the addition of ThT (final concentration: 10 μM). The samples were subjected to the fluorescence intensity measurement (λex = 430 nm, λem = 488 nm), and the kinetic curves were fitted by Eq. (2). At the end of incubation, the resultant product was diluted by PB (10 mM, pH 7.4), 10 μL of which was dropped on the Formvar carbon coated copper-mesh grids and dried at the room temperature. After that, the grids were washed by deionized water for 3 times, added with phosphotungstic acid (2%, w/v) and dried at the room temperature. Finally, the grids were subjected to the TEM observation.

For imaging α-syn-F in vitro, α-syn-F (100 μM, 30 μL) was diluted by 240 μL PB (10 mM, pH 7.4) with the addition of 30 μL NR-CDs (140 μg/mL), and incubated for 40 min, which were the stained fibrils. For comparison, 30 μL NR (140 μg/mL) and Congo red (100 μg/mL) instead of NR-CDs were also studied. Then, the anti-photobleaching property of NR-CDs, NR and Congo red was further compared. Typically, 10 μL of each stained fibrils was sucked out and then dropped on the microslide for drying at the room temperature. Afterwards, all the samples were monitored under an inverted fluorescence microscope with continuous irradiation under the green channel (λex = 528–553 nm), and the images were captured at the fixed time interval (0 min, 1 min, 5 min, 10 min, 15 min, 30 min, 60 min). To fully test the photobleaching resistance of NR-CDs in physiological conditions, the anti-photobleaching property of NR-CDs were also studied in the other three solutions, i.e. 0.9% NaCl, M9 buffer and DMEM.

Next, the imaging ability of NR-CDs towards α-syn aggregates in cell was studied using rotenone-induced α-syn aggregation in SH-SY5Y cells as the model. SH-SY5Y cells were firstly seeded in a 24-well plate at the density of 5 × 104 cells/well, incubating in DMEM culture medium containing 12% fetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37℃ and 5% CO2 for 12 h. Afterwards, rotenone (200 μM) was added to the medium, and incubated with NR-CDs (14 μg/mL) simultaneously with ThT (10 μM), respectively. The cells were observed and photographed with a fluorescence microscope at 0, 4, 8, 12 and 24 h for real-time tracking.

To investigate the imaging ability of NR-CDs for α-syn aggregates in vivo, the transgenic Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) strain NL5901 were used as the studied model. Typically, C. elegans NL5901 were cultured at 20℃ on nematode growth medium (NGM) agar plates seeded with Escherichia coli strain OP50 as food. After reaching the adult stage, the nematodes were washed by the M9 buffer for three times. Then, the obtained NL5901 nematodes were co-incubated with NR-CDs (7 μg/mL) in the M9 buffer for 1 h, then washed thrice by M9 buffer and anesthetized with levamisole on a 3% agarose pad for further fluorescence observation.

All the other experimental information, including materials and reagents, preparation of amyloid fibrils, characterization, investigation of the fluorescence property of NR-CDs in different microenvironments, the dissociation constant (Kd) measurement, CCK-8 assay and the cell uptake experiment, can be found in the supporting materials.

NR is a hydrophobic dye with classic D-π-A structure, in which diethylamine serves as an electron donor and quinone serves as an electron acceptor (Fig. S1A) [27]. It was reported that NR exhibits enhanced fluorescence intensity after binding with protein aggregates for the increased hydrophobicity [24]. However, the poor water solubility of NR hampers its further application, especially in imaging amyloid fibrils in vivo. Fortunately, our previous study has demonstrated that it is effective to improve the properties of the dye probe by generating CDs [22]. Enlightened by this idea, we herein attempted to nano-derivatize NR with citric acid to form CDs, expecting to improve the hydrophilicity and meanwhile maintain the sensing property of the NR dye.

To obtain the optimal turn-on fluorescence response of NR-CDs towards amyloid fibrils, the amount of citric acid at a fixed NR amount was firstly optimized as listed in Table S1. As depicted in Fig. 1A, when the content of citric acid was increased from 0.02 to 1.00 g, the turn-on fluorescence intensity of the resulting CDs was firstly enhanced and then decreased when interacting with α-syn-F, where the highest fluorescence enhancement occurred at the citric acid content of 0.21 g. The reason might be that the residues on the surface of the CDs partly originated from NR, which were supposed to respond to the α-syn-F. In the case of excessive citric acid, the relative residual groups of NR may be reduced on the surface of the CDs, thus causing unfavorable fluorescence response. Therefore, the amount of 0.21 g of citric acid was selected as the optimal to prepare CDs. Furthermore, the effect of reaction time on the turn-on fluorescence performance of CDs towards α-syn-F was investigated. As shown in Fig. 1B, when the reaction time was increased from 4 to 12 h, both the λmax ex and λmax em of the resulting CDs maintained constant to be 545 nm and 630 nm, respectively, but the turn-on fluorescence intensity of NR-CDs co-incubating with α-syn-F reached the maximum at 12 h. Hence, 12 h was chosen as the optimal reaction time.

Overall, based on the above investigations, the optimal synthetic conditions of NR-CDs were determined, i.e. citric acid (0.21 g/mL, 1 mL H2O) and NR (1 mM, 4 mL EtOH) reacted at 180℃ for 12 h. The obtained NR-CDs were used for the following studies.

The morphology and functional groups of NR-CDs were further characterized. As imaged by TEM in Fig. 2A, NR-CDs were spherical with narrowed dispersity, and the average size was estimated to be 2.29 ± 0.03 nm. In addition, the high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 2A, inset) disclosed that NR-CDs had the typical crystalline structure with an interlayer lattice spacing of 0.18 nm.

FT-IR and XPS were employed to analyze the functional groups and chemical composition of NR-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2B, NR-CDs shared the same typical functional groups with both NR (e.g. C-H (2930 cm−1), CC (1625 cm−1), C-O (1250 cm−1), CO (1720 cm−1) and C-C/C-N (1113 cm−1)) and citric acid (e.g. O-H (3440 cm−1), C-H (2930 cm−1) and CO (1720 cm−1)) [28], indicating that NR-CDs might inherit the plentiful functional groups of both NR and citric acid.

In the XPS full scan spectrum of NR-CDs (Fig. 2C-I), the peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.7 eV and 532.3 eV belonged to C1s (∼64.08%), N1s (∼3.21%) and O1s (∼32.71%), respectively. To be specific, in high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2C-II), the signals of C-C/CC (284.6 eV), C-O (285.0–285.1 eV), CO (286.2–286.6 eV) and O-CO (288.0–288.9 eV) were detected [27]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2C-III) disclosed the presence of both CO (532.0 eV) and C-O (533.3 eV) [29]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C-IV) manifested the pyridine nitrogen structure (C-N-C, 399.6 eV) and N-H (401.5 eV) group [22]. The XPS results further verified that NR-CDs preserved most functionalities of NR, especially the unsaturated structure (e.g. CC, CO and CN-C). Considering that NR was lack of a primary or secondary amine group while the N-H groups appeared in NR-CDs, we inferred that the N-H groups may originate from the tertiary amine groups in the side chain of NR during carbonization.

The 1H NMR was further performed to investigate the atom of H in NR-CDs. Eight types of H in NR with the chemical shift (δ) ranging from 6 to 9 ppm were disclosed in Fig. S1B, while five kinds of H with δ of 8.604, 8.271, 7.654, 7.625 and 6.405 ppm were also present in the 1H NMR spectrum of NR-CDs (Fig. S1C). Specifically, the four H atoms ranging from 7.625 to 8.604 ppm in NR-CDs were assigned to the benzene ring, and the H at 6.405 ppm in NR-CDs came from the H atom in NR (OC-CH=C). Reasonably, it can be inferred that, despite some changes occurred, most groups of NR were retained during carbonization to form NR-CDs, coinciding with the results of FT-IR and XPS characterization.

Besides, the optical property of NR-CDs was studied in Fig. S2. The maximum absorption peak of NR-CDs occurred at ∼520 nm, which was red shifted ∼10 nm compared to that of NR (∼510 nm) (Fig. S2A and B). Similarly, both λmax ex and λmax em of NR-CDs (545 nm/630 nm) also red shifted compared to NR (530 nm/610 nm). By calculation, NR-CDs owned faint fluorescence with the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 5.7 %, which was stronger than NR (QY=0.26 %) in water. In addition, Fig. S2C and D showed that NR-CDs exhibited almost excitation independent emission similar to NR. Besides, the fluorescence intensity of NR-CDs was almost irrelevant to pHs during 4–10 with the RSDs of 0.11 %-2.18 %, suggesting its good pH stability (Fig. S2E). Also, NR-CDs exhibited excellent stabilities in 0.9% NaCl, PB (10 mM, pH 7.4), M9 buffer and DMEM, with the RSDs of fluorescence intensity of 4.6 %, 7.6 %, 8.5 % and 8.9 % in 60 min, respectively (Fig. S2F).

Overall, the above characterizations disclosed that NR-CDs retained the most important functional groups, e.g. CC, CO and C-N-C, basically from NR. This could be the main reason that NR-CDs exhibited the turn-on fluorescence response towards the amyloid fibrils as discussed below.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Probing of different amyloid fibrils and monomers by NR-CDs in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Herein, the fluorescence responses of NR-CDs towards different amyloid fibrils were investigated. As demonstrated in Fig. 3A, no fluorescence enhancement was detected when NR interacted with the amyloid monomers. By contrast, its fluorescence turned on to different extents once NR interacted with the amyloid fibrils, in the weakened order of α-syn-F, lys-F, ins-F and Aβ-F. Meanwhile, NR-CDs behaved similar turn-on fluorescence responses towards fibrils with the same order of NR in terms of the fluorescence enhancement tendency (Fig. 3B), suggesting that NR-CDs might inherit the fluorescence property of NR. Since the most favorable turn-on fluorescence was obtained towards α-syn-F compared to other fibrils, α-syn-F was herein selected as the model fibril for the following studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of probing conditions towards α-syn-F by NR-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to obtain the best turn-on signal of NR-CDs when probing α-syn-F, the concentration of NR-CDs was optimized. As depicted in Fig. S3A, when the concentration of NR-CDs was enhanced from 1.4 to 140 μg/mL, the fluorescence intensity firstly increased (1.4–14 μg/mL), and then reached the platform and maintained almost unchanged (14–140 μg/mL). The reason might be that, corresponding to the fixed concentration of α-syn-F, the more availability of NR-CDs was, the stronger fluorescence enhancement would be resulted in. When the binding sites of NR-CDs were completely occupied by the fibrils, the excessive NR-CDs could not cause enhancement of the fluorescence intensity, leading to the unchanged fluorescence intensity with further elevated concentration of NR-CDs. Therefore, 14 μg/mL of NR-CDs was selected as the optimal concentration to probe α-syn-F, and the corresponding excitation and emission spectra in the presence of α-syn-F are shown in Fig. S3B.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of turn-on fluorescence probing fibrils by NR-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been known that the formation of insoluble amyloid fibrils would induce the change of microenvironment, presenting the lowered polarity and further the deepened extent of aggregation, resulting in more viscous microenvironment. Based on this fundamental, a diversity of microenvironment-sensitive probes aiming at the amyloid fibrils were designed and constructed [30]. NR is a typical solvatochromistic dye, which is classical for the fat staining [25,31]. Based on the characterization in Section 3.2, NR-CDs may maintain most functional groups of NR. Thus, it is expected that NR-CDs may also be sensitive to the polarity and viscosity of microenvironment.

As depicted in Fig. 4A and 4C, with the enhancing glycerol ratio in water-glycerol binary solvents representing elevated viscosity, the fluorescence intensities of both NR-CDs and NR strengthened gradually. To be noted that, as the water solubility of NR was quite limited [24], the investigated concentration of NR in final solution was only 0.14 μg/mL; while, NR-CDs owned much better water dispersibility, and herein the concentration of NR-CDs was 14 μg/mL in an agreement with the optimal concentration for detection. Obviously, the stronger fluorescence intensity was observed in the case of NR-CDs, which could profit from the enhanced water dispersibility and thus the possibility of using higher concentration of the NR-CDs compared to NR. Meantime, the λmax ex and λmax em of NR-CDs red shifted from ∼545 nm to 580 nm and ∼630 nm to 650 nm, respectively (Fig. S4). Furthermore, water and nine organic solvents were used to study the influence of polarity on the fluorescence intensity of NR-CDs. As revealed in Fig. 4B and 4D, the fluorescence intensities of NR-CDs and NR in the nine studied organic solvents were all stronger than that in water, corresponding to the strongest polarity of water. Particularly, it was reported that CH2Cl2 could mimic the β-sheet structure of the amyloid fibrils [32]. As measured, the fluorescence intensity of NR-CDs strengthened greatly in CH2Cl2 (Fig. 4B) with the QY of 93.1%, almost 16-fold larger than that in water (5.7%), implying that NR-CDs could effectively bind with the β-sheet structure of the fibrils. Overall, the above phenomena fully proved that, similar to NR, NR-CDs were sensitive to the microenvironment with different viscosity and polarity, as well as the β-sheet of the fibrils.

The reason why both NR and NR-CDs respond differently towards different amyloid fibrils could be owing to various interactions between them. Firstly, the β-sheet hydrophobic structure, the primary region to bind with the probes, differs in different proteins, resulting in differential response. Secondly, in addition to hydrophobic interaction, hydrogen bond and electrostatic interactions may also exist between the fibrils and the probes. On the one hand, the hydrogen bond exists between the amino acid residues in the fibrils and the -COOH and/or -NH2 on the surface of CDs. Hence, the difference in the protein residues should be responsible for the difference in the hydrogen bond. On the other hand, electrostatic force, including electrostatic attraction or repulsion force, exist between the charged CDs and charged proteins. The isoelectric point (pI) values of the studied proteins are different, i.e. 4.67, 5.30, 5.35 and 11.30 for α-syn, Aβ, ins and lys, respectively. The NR-CDs were negative in water and PB (10 mM, pH 7.4) media with the zeta potential of − 21.8 and − 42.6 mV, respectively. Hence, electrostatic repulsion could exist between NR-CDs and α-syn, Aβ and ins, which were negatively charged at pH 7.4; while, lys was positively charged and the electrostatic attraction force would exist. Based on the above analysis, NR-CDs might exert different interaction forces on different fibrils, which may be the reason why the fluorescence turned on differently.

To further evaluate the concrete binding affinity of NR-CDs to the α-syn-F, the dissociation constant (K d) value between them was determined using NR and Congo red for comparison. As shown in Fig. S5, the NR-CDs had the highest turn-on fluorescence intensity towards α-syn-F, NR in the second and Congo red in the last. However, the K d values were measured to be increased in the order of Congo red (1.735 μg/mL), NR-CDs (1.877 μg/mL) and NR (2.148 μg/mL), indicating the weakened binding affinity with α-syn-F in this order. Specifically, compared to NR, NR-CDs possessed slightly stronger affinity with α-syn-F, suggesting that the carbonization of NR to form CDs may promote the affinity to the fibrils, possibly due to more availability of binding sites profiting from the improved hydrophilicity. Anyway, these three K d values were in the same magnitude to each other, indicating comparable binding affinities of the studied probes towards α-syn-F. Thus, the selectivity and sensitivity of NR-CDs when probing the amyloid fibrils would be guaranteed.

To sum, during the formation of the amyloid fibrils, both the microenvironmental polarity and viscosity were changed, which could be probed by NR-CDs. In addition, NR-CDs had comparable affinity with Congo red and NR to the fibrils via different interactions, e.g. hydrophobic interaction, hydrogen bond and electrostatic force. Therefore, the designed NR-CDs had the great potential to probe α-syn-F with the remarkable turn-on fluorescence response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro monitoring aggregation of α-syn using NR-CDs as the probe
ce:section.ce:para: Monitoring the amyloid fibrillation process is of great importance to get a deep understanding of the occurrence and development of the related diseases. As discussed above, α-syn-F could significantly turn on the fluorescence of NR-CDs while α-syn could not. Herein, the ability of NR-CDs as the probe to monitor the aggregation of α-syn was investigated, using ThT for comparison. In Fig. 5, although weaker fluorescence enhancement extent was detected by the NR-CDs than the ThT probe, similar aggregation curves were obtained, indicating that NR-CDs owned the comparable ability to monitor the aggregation of α-syn. Furthermore, t lag values identified by NR-CDs and ThT were calculated to be the same as 48 h. Besides, t 1/2 was calculated as 28.35 h and 28.66 h for NR-CDs and ThT, respectively, which were almost identical to each other, further suggesting the effectiveness of NR-CDs for in vitro monitoring amyloid aggregation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the inhibition effect of NR-CDs on amyloid aggregation
ce:section.ce:para: One of the most noticeable abilities of the CDs when it comes to the researches related to amyloid fibrils is to inhibit amyloid aggregation, which could potentially delay or suppress the occurrence of the related diseases [20]. For this reason, digging the inhibitory potential of NR-CDs may hopefully provide a possible way against the disease. The ThT fluorescence assay and TEM assay were herein employed to investigate the inhibition effect of NR-CDs on the aggregation of α-syn. As demonstrated in Fig. 6A, when NR-CDs were co-incubated with α-syn, the ThT assay disclosed that the formation of α-syn-F was largely suppressed. Specifically, at the ending point of the incubation, the ThT fluorescence intensity of the α-syn + NR-CDs group was much weaker than that of the α-syn group (only 30%); meanwhile, the final product of the α-syn + NR-CDs group was observed as small fragments, but the mature fibrils were present in the case of the α-syn group (Fig. 6B and 6C). The mechanism underlying the anti-aggregation property of NR-CDs against α-syn might be related to various interactions between them as discussed in Section 3.5. These observations demonstrated that NR-CDs could potentially inhibit the formation of amyloid fibrils, and hold a promise as an effective inhibitor against the α-syn aggregation.

The cytotoxicity of NR-CDs was investigated via the CCK-8 assay. As presented in Fig. S6, despite NR had almost no cytotoxicity at low concentration (≤14 μg/mL), it showed serious cytotoxicity at high concentration (>70 μg/mL) with the cell viability below 10%. As a contrast, the cell viability remained above 70% even at a high concentration of NR-CDs up to 700 μg/mL. Obviously, the cytotoxicity of the NR-CDs was greatly lessened compared to its carbon source (i.e. NR) at a high concentration above 70 μg/mL, implying its potential as the appropriate turn-on fluorescence probe for imaging amyloid fibrils in vivo.

In light of the good water compatibility and improved cytotoxicity of NR-CDs compared to the NR dye, the imaging performance of NR-CDs was further investigated. Firstly, the anti-photobleaching performance of NR-CDs in four physiological conditions, i.e. 0.9 % NaCl, M9 buffer, DMEM and PB (10 mM, pH 7.4), were investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, apparent red fluorescence of α-syn aggregates stained by NR-CDs remained stable upon 60 min continuous irradiation, suggesting that NR-CDs possessed excellent anti-photobleaching property across various physiologically relevant solutions. Additionally, the property of photobleaching resistance of NR-CDs was compared with two dyes, i.e. NR and Cong red, in PB. These three probes all showed obvious turn-on red fluorescence when binding with the α-syn-F. After subjected to the continuous irradiation, it could be clearly seen that the red fluorescence of the α-syn-F labeled with Congo red and NR was sharply declined after 30 min irradiation, and almost faded at 60 min. Superiorly, the fluorescence of the α-syn-F marked by NR-CDs remained almost constant when exposed to the 60 min continuous irradiation, proving that NR-CDs had better photobleaching resistance compared to NR and Congo red. In addition, the cell uptake rate of NR-CDs was measured to reach 19.4%, further demonstrating its potential to image α-syn-F in vivo.

In light of the good performance of NR-CDs towards α-syn-F as discussed above, it was applied to image α-syn-F in the typical PD cell model, the rotenone stimulated SH-SY5Y. Rotenone was reported as a common reagent to induce the production of α-syn aggregates [33]. As shown in Fig. 7, green dotted fluorescence from ThT was observed in green channel at 4 h and reached maximal brightness at 8 h, suggesting the successful formation of α-syn-F. Meanwhile, in the red channel, α-syn-F emitted red fluorescence due to the staining by NR-CDs, which was coincident with ThT. From this, we can see that the NR-CDs could be used to real-time monitoring the aggregation of α-syn within living cells, herein SH-SY5Y.

Furthermore, the typical PD model NL5901 C. elegans expressing α-syn-YFP in muscle cells was used to test the imaging ability of NR-CDs for α-syn aggregates in vivo. As disclosed in Fig. 8, the green fluorescence of α-syn aggregates derived from the labeled YFP was observed at both the head and body region in NL5901 nematode [34], which merged well with the red fluorescence resulting from the NR-CDs imaging. The good fluorescence colocalization of NR-CDs with YFP also demonstrated the excellent imaging ability of our NR-CDs for α-syn aggregates in vivo.

In this study, Nile red (NR) was nano-derivatized to be CDs, i.e. NR-based CDs (NR-CDs). Compared with NR, the proposed NR-CDs not only maintained the excellent turn-on red fluorescence detection performance towards α-synuclein fibril, but also performed outstandingly in the aspects of good water dispersibility, strong photobleaching resistance, low cytotoxicity and high binding affinity to amyloid fibrils. Moreover, the anti-aggregation ability of NR-CDs for α-syn was also demonstrated, which seemed exciting for the theranostic purpose. Overall, the present study provided an effective way of designing the CDs using the dye as the carbon source, to improve the biocompatibility and fluorescence properties while retain the selective turn-on fluorescence response towards the target. This way may be generalized to construct more fluorescence probe with desired properties aiming at amyloid fibrils.

Li Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Jin-Tao Zhang: Methodology. li xu: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Li-Yun Ma: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Kun Huang: Supervision. Xu Yu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Qin-Ying Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology. Lin-Jiao Yang: Methodology. Zhi Li: Methodology. Ming Jiang: Methodology. Wan-Chun Luo: Methodology. Xi Li: Methodology. Yu Zhang: Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Li Xu reports financial support was provided by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team. Li Xu reports financial support was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China. Xu YU reports financial support was provided by the National Natural Science Foundation of China. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Program for HUST Academic Frontier Youth Team (No. 2019QYTD09), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 82373834 & 22174049). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for the material characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135785.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Dr. Qin-ying Li received her Ph.D. degree from Huazhong University of Science and Technology in 2022. Now, she works in Department of Pharmacy, Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. Her research topic focuses on the novel fluorescent nanomaterial for diagnosis and treatment of neurodegenerative diseases.

Prof. Kun Huang is a full professor in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong Uni-versity of Science and Technology, China. He received his PhD degree from CaseWestern Reserve University in 2006. His research interests focus on mechanisticstudy and therapy of metabolic diseases.

Xu Yu received his Ph.D. degree from Wuhan University in 2013. He worked as a postdoctoral researcher at the Department of Biomedical Engineering, Pennsylvania State University, USA and the Department of Chemistry, Hebrew University of Jerusalem, Israel, respectively. Currently, he is an associate professor in the Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. His current research focused on microfluidic chip, biosensors, and nanotechnology.

Prof. Li Xu is a full professor in Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, China. She received her PhD degree from National University of Singapore in 2009. Her research interests focus on separation and analysis of complex samples and application of functional materials in pharmaceutical, medical and environmental analysis.
